title: Criticism of proposed changes to superannuation laws that allow perpetrators to financially benefit
full_text:
Sarah* has spent the past few months grieving the death of her daughter Mary*, who died in a car accident as she was fleeing domestic violence.
Her distress has been amplified after discovering that the perpetrator of that abuse could financially benefit from her daughter's death.
"As more and more evidence was coming forward of the abuse, I was feeling sick to the stomach that someone who had abused my daughter so badly and lied and manipulated so dramatically could actually benefit financially," she said.
Due to an ongoing police investigation into the domestic abuse allegations, the ABC cannot use their real names.
Mary, who was in her 20s, had superannuation split across two funds.
When Sarah contacted these funds to ask them to freeze her daughter's accounts, she said she was told it wasn't possible.
"They couldn't actually freeze any activity; they could allow me to make a claim, but he [the perpetrator] could also make a claim."
Superannuation laws mean death benefits can be paid to a person who perpetrated family or domestic violence against the deceased, despite evidence of abuse.
When a person dies, their superannuation and any associated insurance cover are paid out to their dependants, namely a spouse/de facto, children, or nominated beneficiary, through a legally binding or non-binding nomination.
Sarah said that while her daughter had not listed her perpetrator as a beneficiary, she also did not have a binding nomination in place.
"[The super fund] explained to me that a de facto would be considered for the claim first and foremost because there were no children involved in this situation," Sarah said.
Because Sarah was still listed on the rental where her abuser was living, he could still be seen as her de facto.
She also said that neither fund provided a clear avenue for disclosing the abuse.
"The questions on the forms are simply, 'Is there a domestic partner?'" she said.
"Leaving a domestic violence situation is more often than not incredibly difficult … it is not as simple as the question on the claim form of whether there was a domestic partner or not."
Current system's 'failings'
The federal government is reviewing superannuation laws following industry stakeholders' highlighting failings in the system during a 2024 parliamentary inquiry.
Treasury released a consultation paper with three new policy options in March. Industry advocates and experts had until mid-April to provide feedback.
It has sparked mixed reactions about what best practice looks like and highlighted possible complications when considering reform.
The first proposal by the federal government would allow a super fund to overrule a binding death benefit nomination if it believes "on a fair and reasonable basis" the chosen beneficiary has perpetrated abuse.
The two other proposed options would require the courts to determine whether domestic violence was present in the relationship before a super fund could decide death benefit payments.
Reform doesn't consider nuance of DV: advocates
Jasmine Opdam from the Redfern Legal Centre says involving the courts is unacceptable.
"Those options are based on a misapprehension that every victim-survivor of family violence will have a court order confirming this, and it assumes that court and police records are the only forms of evidence of family violence," she said.
"All you have to do is look at the attrition rates as cases go through the criminal justice system and see how few reports of family violence actually go to court, let alone result in convictions."
She also said it placed all the onus on the family.
"You're asking someone who has just lost a loved one … you're asking them to then also go and retain a lawyer and pay tens of thousands, maybe hundreds of thousands of dollars in legal fees to go through expensive and time-consuming court proceedings, which might actually eat up most of the value of the superannuation anyway," Ms Opdam said.
Redfern Legal Centre made a joint submission to the federal government, alongside industry stakeholders including Super Consumers Australia.
They are recommending legislation that requires all super funds to consider domestic and family violence when deciding death benefit claims, and ensuring funds have the power to set aside eligible beneficiaries if there is persuasive evidence of violence.
Involving the courts could make processes worse for everyone involved, according to Lily Jiang, the director of advocacy at Super Consumer Australia.
"It would mean taking years and years and years to make these decisions, and the super funds are already being put on notice for their poor customer service when it comes to death benefit claims, insurance claims," she said.
"We don't want a solution that actually makes things even take even longer than is required to actually close out the division."
Ms Jiang said it was about "leveraging the strengths of the current system".
"[Super funds are] well-practised at navigating these things and making a decision on who the right people should be to pay the entitlement to, that's their obligation to their members," she said.
"We want to leverage that strength, but require the law to be changed to require super funds to take into account domestic family violence when they make death benefit decisions."
Super industry split on whether courts should be involved
On the other hand, one peak body for the super industry said the judicial process was essential when deciding if abuse was perpetrated to ensure procedural fairness.
"Death benefits are legal entitlements that belong to beneficiaries under trustee law. They are not discretionary payments. Stripping someone of a legal entitlement requires an objective, legally defensible finding, particularly where criminal conduct is alleged," said Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia's (ASFA's) CEO Mary Delahunty in a statement to the ABC.
ASFA represents retail, industry, corporate, and public-sector super funds.
"Unlike judicial bodies, super fund trustees do not have investigative powers or mechanisms to test contested claims about criminal conduct in a procedurally fair way," Ms Delahunty said.
"ASFA's position is that funds should have discretion to disqualify a person from receiving death benefits, but only where a court or another properly constituted tribunal has made a relevant finding."
Professor of finance at the UTS Business School, Kathy Walsh, argues that while involving the courts makes it easier for funds to make a "clearer decision that's not going to be challenged", ultimately, the burden of proof is "too high".
"If you talked to a lot of victim survivors, [they would argue] the court process is not a fair process for many of them anyway," she said.
Dealing with complex family matters isn't new to super funds, according to Professor Walsh.
"Superannuation companies, trustees often have to make these decisions … they do look at a binding nomination that's been, for example, written too long ago or paid to adult children so it's no longer viable."
She believes it's important that funds have the authority to assess complex family matters when it comes to domestic violence, too, but what that evidence looks like is still unclear.
Courts 'expensive and lengthy'
Another industry stakeholder who has commented on the reforms is the peak body for profit-to-member super funds.
In a statement to the ABC, Super Members Council CEO Misha Schubert said she also did not believe involving the courts was best practice.
"We don't support pushing cases into expensive and lengthy court processes that risk re-traumatising families and delaying justice," she said.
"Super trustees already deal with complex family circumstances, and with clear laws backing them to do so, they can make fair, evidence‑based decisions acting in the interests of the member who has died, without forcing grieving loved ones into adversarial court battles with perpetrators."
In a statement to the ABC, Assistant Treasurer and Minister for Financial Services Daniel Mulino said the Albanese government had consulted on several policy options to prevent perpetrators of family and domestic violence from accessing victims' death benefits.
"We are now considering the outcomes of those consultations," he said.
"The government will progress a final policy that emphasises the need to ensure that victims' families are not re-traumatised by complex and inaccessible processes, and that the need for certainty and security for all parties is paramount."
Calls to introduce domestic violence training
Outside of the court process, Super Consumers Australia is recommending mandatory superannuation service standards that include training and capacity to identify and support members experiencing domestic and family violence.
"We need to have super funds be required to develop their capability, develop their ability and protections to support victims, survivors and their families, not just for death benefits, but across all their products and services," Ms Jiang said.
Ms Opdam says it should already have been implemented.
"Superannuation funds are definitely lagging behind the rest of the financial services sector," she said.
"These funds should be training their staff to handle these cases appropriately and provide the necessary support to their customers.
"We expect that of banks, we expect that of many other financial service providers and telecommunications providers now are legally required to do that, so there's no reason [super funds] shouldn't be in the same category."
Sarah agrees and said she hoped new laws in this space would mean other families were not further traumatised like her.
"It's taken away so much of my time and energy, leaving me feeling that I have not had the chance to grieve my daughter properly," she said.
"Instead, I have been going through and finding all this information myself, googling and searching, and liaising with solicitors."
Australian Broadcasting Corporation
Document ABCNEW0020260507em57001be
summary: Proposed changes to superannuation laws in Australia could allow perpetrators of domestic violence to benefit financially from the death of their victims. Current laws permit death benefits to be paid to individuals without considering evidence of abuse, prompting calls for reforms that would allow super funds to disregard claims from abusers. Stakeholders are divided on whether court involvement is necessary for determining claims related to domestic violence.

heading: Superannuation and Investments
key_points: Superannuation laws currently allow abusers to claim death benefits despite evidence of domestic violence.
Proposed reforms include allowing super funds to overrule binding nominations based on evidence of abuse.
There is debate over the necessity of court involvement in determining claims related to domestic violence.
relevance: These developments impact how superannuation funds manage death benefit claims, which is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals involved in risk assessment and compliance.
